label: Bereshit — Part 1 — Chapters 1–11
summary_plain: Bereshit Part 1 tells the beginning. The Creator makes the heavens, the earth, and every living thing in six days and rests on the seventh. The first humans, Adam and Chavah, live in a garden called Eden. They are given one rule — do not eat from the tree of the knowledge of good and evil — and they break it. Their sons Qayin and Hevel bring offerings to YHWH; Qayin kills Hevel. Generations pass. Chanokh walks so closely with The Creator that The Creator takes him without death. Noakh and his family are the only humans saved when YHWH sends a flood to cleanse the earth. After the flood The Creator sets his bow in the cloud as the sign of a covenant never to destroy the earth by water again. Humanity builds a tower at Bavel, and YHWH confuses their language and scatters them across the earth.
summary_scholarly: Chapters 1 through 11 run from the creation of the heavens and the earth to the scattering of humanity at Bavel. The ACR reads this material directly from two living primary witnesses: the Ethiopian Orit Ge'ez — kept continuously by the African Israelite community through millennia without interruption — and the Dead Sea Scrolls at Qumran (4QGen-a through 4QGen-j, Cave 4). The Orit is the living witness that preserved the text, and the Qumran scrolls attest the same text in the land itself. The Masoretic is a later variant. It is not a baseline. Where it differs from the Orit and the Qumran readings, the difference is noted at the end of each chapter and the Orit / Qumran reading is kept as the main text. What this produces is concrete. YHWH / 𐤉𐤄𐤅𐤄 is the personal name and it is preserved as the personal name. Elohim is rendered here as The Creator. There is no 'LORD' and no 'God' — those are European substitutions imposed on the text through Greek, then Latin, then English. Personal names are in their original Hebrew: Qayin, Hevel, Chanokh, Metushelakh, Noakh, Bavel. The Chanokh narrative at chapter 5 — 'he walked with The Creator and was not, for The Creator took him' — opens directly into the full Book of Chanokh, preserved complete in the Orit Ge'ez and attested in the Dead Sea Scrolls. It is primary scripture. The ACR reproduces Chanokh as Volume 6. The Nephilim and the Watchers named briefly in chapter 6 are told in full in that same Chanokh text and in Yovelim (Book of Jubilees), also preserved in the Orit Ge'ez and attested at Qumran, reproduced as ACR Volume 7.

key_terms.definition: Hebrew for 'in the beginning' — the first word of the book, and the Hebrew name for what European Bibles later called Genesis.
'Without form and void' (1:2). The state of the earth before The Creator's ordering work of the six days.
Spirit or breath. 'The Spirit of The Creator was hovering over the face of the waters' (1:2).
The first human. Formed 'of dust from the ground' (2:7). The name shares its root with adamah — Hebrew for 'ground'.
The first woman. The name means 'living' — 'mother of all living' (3:20). Anglicized in European Bibles as Eve.
Adam and Chavah's first two sons (chapter 4). Qayin killed Hevel after YHWH received their offerings differently. Anglicized in European Bibles as Cain and Abel.

The seventh from Adam in the line of Shet. 'He walked with The Creator and was not, for The Creator took him' (5:24). The full Book of Chanokh is preserved complete in the Orit Ge'ez of the African Israelite community and attested in multiple Qumran scrolls. ACR reproduces it as Volume 6, Parts 1 through 3.
Chanokh's son. Lived 969 years — the longest lifespan recorded in the generations before the flood. Anglicized as Methuselah.
The patriarch of the flood narrative (chapters 6 through 9). Righteous in his generation. Noakh walked with The Creator. The ancestor of every human after the flood. Anglicized as Noah.
The city and tower (chapter 11) where YHWH confused the languages of humanity. The Hebrew plays on balal, 'to confuse'. Anglicized through Greek and Latin as Babel, later as Babylon.

fill_blank.prompt: In the beginning The Creator created the ______ and the earth.
fill_blank.source_quote: In the beginning The Creator created the heavens and the earth.

fill_blank.prompt: The earth was without form and ______, and darkness was over the face of the deep.
fill_blank.source_quote: The earth was without form and void, and darkness was over the face of the deep. And the Spirit of The Creator was hovering over the face of the waters.

fill_blank.prompt: And The Creator said: Let there be ______, and there was ______.
fill_blank.source_quote: And The Creator said: Let there be light, and there was light.

fill_blank.prompt: So The Creator created man in his own ______.
fill_blank.source_quote: So The Creator created man in his own image, in the image of The Creator he created him — male and female he created them.

fill_blank.prompt: Then YHWH The Creator formed the man of dust from the ground and breathed into his nostrils the breath of ______.
fill_blank.source_quote: Then YHWH The Creator formed the man of dust from the ground and breathed into his nostrils the breath of life, and the man became a living creature.

fill_blank.prompt: I do not know — am I my brother's ______?
fill_blank.source_quote: Then YHWH said to Qayin: Where is Hevel your brother? He said: I do not know — am I my brother's keeper?

fill_blank.prompt: Chanokh walked with The Creator, and he was ______, for The Creator took him.
fill_blank.source_quote: Chanokh walked with The Creator, and he was not, for The Creator took him.

fill_blank.prompt: On that day all the ______ of the great deep burst forth, and the windows of the heavens were opened.
fill_blank.source_quote: In the six hundredth year of Noakh's life, in the second month, on the seventeenth day of the month, on that day all the fountains of the great deep burst forth, and the windows of the heavens were opened.

fill_blank.prompt: I have set my ______ in the cloud, and it shall be a sign of the covenant between me and the earth.
fill_blank.source_quote: I have set my bow in the cloud, and it shall be a sign of the covenant between me and the earth.

fill_blank.prompt: Therefore its name was called Bavel, because there YHWH confused the ______ of all the earth.
fill_blank.source_quote: Therefore its name was called Bavel, because there YHWH confused the language of all the earth. And from there YHWH dispersed them over the face of all the earth.

multiple_choice.question: On which day did The Creator separate the light from the darkness?
multiple_choice.source_quote: And The Creator separated the light from the darkness. The Creator called the light Day, and the darkness he called Night. And there was evening and there was morning — the first day.

multiple_choice.question: What did The Creator call the dry land?
multiple_choice.source_quote: The Creator called the dry land Earth, and the waters that were gathered together he called Seas.

multiple_choice.question: Which tree was Adam forbidden to eat from?
options: The tree of the knowledge of good and evil
multiple_choice.source_quote: But of the tree of the knowledge of good and evil you shall not eat, for in the day that you eat of it you shall surely die.

multiple_choice.question: Who were Adam and Chavah's first two sons?
multiple_choice.source_quote: Now Adam knew Chavah his wife, and she conceived and bore Qayin... And again, she bore his brother Hevel.

multiple_choice.question: Which patriarch lived 365 years and was taken by The Creator without dying?
multiple_choice.source_quote: Thus all the days of Chanokh were 365 years. Chanokh walked with The Creator, and he was not, for The Creator took him.

multiple_choice.question: How many of each kind of clean animal did Noakh take onto the ark?
multiple_choice.source_quote: Take with you seven pairs of all clean animals, the male and his mate, and a pair of the animals that are not clean, the male and his mate.

multiple_choice.question: How long did the rain of the flood fall on the earth?
multiple_choice.source_quote: And rain fell upon the earth forty days and forty nights.

multiple_choice.question: What did The Creator set in the cloud as the sign of his covenant with Noakh?
multiple_choice.source_quote: I have set my bow in the cloud, and it shall be a sign of the covenant between me and the earth.

multiple_choice.question: How does the text describe the Nephilim?
options: The mighty men who were of old, the men of renown
multiple_choice.source_quote: The Nephilim were on the earth in those days, and also afterward, when the sons of The Creator came in to the daughters of man and they bore children to them. These were the mighty men who were of old, the men of renown.

multiple_choice.question: At Bavel, what did YHWH do to stop the building of the tower?
multiple_choice.source_quote: Come, let us go down and there confuse their language, so that they may not understand one another's speech. So YHWH dispersed them from there over the face of all the earth.

faq.question: Why does the ACR say 'The Creator' and 'YHWH' instead of 'God' and 'LORD'?
faq.answer: 'God' is a generic Germanic word that European Bibles substituted for Elohim. 'LORD' in capital letters is a substitution that hides YHWH — the personal name the text itself uses. The Orit Ge'ez and the Dead Sea Scrolls both preserve YHWH as the personal name and Elohim (rendered here as The Creator) as the title. European Bibles replaced YHWH with 'LORD' and Elohim with 'God'. The ACR does not.

faq.question: Why are the names in Hebrew — Qayin, Hevel, Chanokh, Noakh — instead of Cain, Abel, Enoch, Noah?
faq.answer: Those Hebrew forms are the names the text actually uses. The anglicized forms came through Greek, then Latin, then English — three layers of substitution carried by European missionary Bibles. The ACR keeps the names as the manuscripts record them. The anglicized form is noted only so a reader who grew up with a European Bible can recognize which figure is meant.

faq.question: Who was Chanokh and why is his book in ACR Volume 6?
faq.answer: Chanokh was the seventh patriarch from Adam, in the line of Shet. The Bereshit text says he walked with The Creator and The Creator took him — he does not die (5:24). The full Book of Chanokh is preserved complete in the Orit Ge'ez of the African Israelite community and is attested in multiple scrolls from Qumran Cave 4 (4QEn-a through 4QEn-g). It is primary scripture. The African Israelites in Ethiopia and the Qumran community in the land both kept it as scripture continuously. The ACR reproduces it as Volume 6, Parts 1 through 3.

faq.question: Does the ACR use the same chronology as a European Bible for the generations before the flood?
faq.answer: No. The ACR preserves the lifespans as the Orit Ge'ez and the Dead Sea Scrolls record them. The Masoretic was altered in places by hundreds of years per generation. The Masoretic reading is noted at the end of the chapter as a variant, not adopted as the main text.

faq.question: What are the Nephilim in chapter 6?
faq.answer: Chapter 6:4 names the Nephilim — 'the mighty men who were of old, the men of renown' — as the offspring of 'the sons of The Creator' and 'the daughters of man'. The Bereshit account is brief. The full story is told in the Book of Chanokh, specifically the Book of the Watchers, which the ACR reproduces as Volume 6 Part 1. The Watchers are named individually. Their descent to the earth, what they taught humanity, the judgment passed on them, and the flood as The Creator's response to the corruption they caused are all laid out there. This material was preserved continuously by the African Israelite community and found in the Dead Sea Scrolls at Qumran.

faq.question: What does the scattering at Bavel mean?
faq.answer: The tower at Bavel (chapter 11) follows the table of nations in chapter 10. The text records that a unified human language and settlement was broken up — YHWH confused the languages and scattered the people into the many peoples, languages, and territories already named in the preceding chapter. The Hebrew name Bavel plays on the verb balal, 'to confuse'.

faq.question: Where is Eden according to the text itself?
faq.answer: Bereshit 2:13 names the second river of Eden as Gihon — 'it is the one that flowed around the whole land of Cush.' Cush in the DSS, the Orit Ge'ez, and every ancient source is the Ethiopian-Sudanese Nile Valley corridor. The Eden geography is centered in Northeast Africa. The covenant origin narrative is African. The Levantine territory later called Canaan-Judah is the northernmost extension of an African covenant geography — not a separate 'Middle Eastern' civilization. The term 'Middle East' was invented in 1902 by a US naval strategist. It did not exist in any ancient language.

faq.question: What does the Table of Nations (chapter 10) reveal about Shem and Ham?
faq.answer: Bereshit 10:6 places Ham's sons as Cush (Nubia/Ethiopia), Mizraim (Egypt), Put (Libya), and Canaan (the Levantine coast). Shem and Ham are brothers — their territories are adjacent, their populations biologically related. The separation of Semitic populations from African populations in Western academic classification has no basis in the text's own genealogy. The covenant people descend through Shem, whose territory is the Afro-Asiatic corridor from the Euphrates to the Nile. Ancient Hebrew belongs to the Afro-Asiatic language family — the same family as ancient Egyptian, Ge'ez, Amharic, and Somali. The majority of this family's branches are exclusively African.

faq.question: Does the text say anything about the land sitting on the African tectonic plate?
faq.answer: The land west of the Jordan River — including everything called Israel, the Negev, the Galilee, and the coastal plain — sits on the African tectonic plate (the Nubian Plate). The Jordan Rift Valley is the geological boundary between Africa and the Arabian Plate. This is physical geology. Before the Suez Canal was dug in 1869, there was no water barrier separating the Levant from Africa. A person walking from Cairo to Jerusalem never left the African continent. The covenant land is African soil.

faq.question: What does Bereshit 9:27 mean — 'let Japheth dwell in the tents of Shem'?
faq.answer: The Hebrew yishkon means to dwell as a settler, to take up residence in another's household. The text describes Japheth-descended populations inhabiting the covenant spaces that belong to the Shem lineage. Dwelling in — not becoming. The Khazar Correspondence (c. 960 CE, Cambridge University Library) records King Joseph of Khazaria tracing his ancestry to Togarmah, son of Gomer, son of Japheth — the European-Anatolian line, not the Shemitic covenant line. The European populations that have adopted the Hebrew covenant identity since the Khazar conversion occupy the tents of Shem exactly as Bereshit 9:27 describes.
